free_text: Observation of large enhancements of charge exchange
cross sections with neutron-rich carbon isotopes
#doi:10.1093/ptep/ptw034
Two different targets were used: carbon (graphite)
target with thickness of 4.010 g/cm**2, hydrogen
target (polyethylene) with thickness of 3.625 g/cm**2
Multi-sampling ion chamber (MUSIC)
Time-projection chamber (TPC)
Plastic scintillator detector
Before hitting the target, the beam passes through the
following detectors: SCIN-1, TPC-1, MUSIC-1, TPC-2,
and a plastic veto detector. Behind the target the
following detectors were positioned: MUSIC-2, TPC-3,
and SCIN-2
Ar-40 and Ne-22 beams with energy of 1 GeV/u were
used to produce secondary beams of carbon isotopes at
around 950 MeV/u. The production target was a 5 g/cm**2
thick Be plate.
Production of nitrogen isotopes
were studied on proton and carbon targets using
high-energy beams of incident isotopes. In the
experiment, only the nitrogen isotopes were
identified, at angles with near zero degrees. The
incident particles were identified via time-of-flight
and their energy loss. The incident position and
angle of the bombarding particles were determined
with time-projection chambers. A veto detector was
used to reject events for which the incident carbon
was associated with other charged particles. The
charge Z of the outgoing particles from the target
was determined via their energy loss. The position
distribution of Z=7 particles was measured.
The cross section were determined after subtracting
the empty target background. The cross section for a
pure proton target was obtained by subtracting the
carbon target cross section from the that of the
polyethylene target.
No detailed error analysis was provided.
